title: Solve sinusoidal equations (basic)
description: Evaluate solutions to equations of the form sin(x)=d or cos(x)=d where d is any number.
topic_path: Get ready for applications of integration

assessment_items.question_content:
Fien tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in degrees to the nearest tenth, in the interval from $-180\degree$ to $180\degree$, but she made a mistake.

$\sin(x)=0.95$ 

Here is Fien's work.

$\begin{array}{rcl}
\sin^{-1}(\sin (x)) &= &\sin^{-1}( 0.95)\\\\
x &\approx &1.3\\\\
\swarrow&&\searrow\\\\
\begin{align}
&\blueE{\text{Using }\sin(x)=\sin(x+360)}\\\\
x &\approx 1.3+  360n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -358.7\text{, }1.3\text{,}361.3\ldots 
\end{align}&&
\begin{align}
&\purpleD{\text{Using }\sin(x)=\sin(180-x)}\\\\
&\purpleD{\text{and }\sin(x)=\sin(x+360)}\\\\
x &\approx 178.7 + 360 n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -181.3\text{, }178.7\text{, }538.7\ldots 
\end{align}
\end{array}$

>The solutions to $x$ within the interval from $-180\degree$ to $180\degree$ are $1.3\degree$ and $178.7\degree$.

**What was Fien's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Fien needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse sine function $\sin^{-1}$. She also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks her to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving sine equations:

- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(180\degree-\theta)$
- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\theta+360\degree)$

After Fien uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, she should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $-180\degree$ to $180\degree$.

###Isolating $x$

$\sin^{-1}( 0.95)\approx 71.8\degree\approx 1.3\,\text{radians}$

Fien isolated $x$ in radians instead of in degrees.

### Conclusion

Fien evaluated $\sin^{-1}\left( 0.95\right)$ in radians instead of in degrees.

choices.content: Fien evaluated $\sin^{-1}\left( 0.95\right)$ in radians instead of in degrees.
Fien used an identity that only applies to the cosine function, not to the sine function.
Fien forgot to use larger $n$-values which would reveal another solution in the interval.
Fien gave solutions that were outside of the interval.

assessment_items.question_content:
Emrick tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in degrees to the nearest tenth, in the interval from $-360\degree$ to $0\degree$, but he made a mistake.

$\cos(x)=-0.13$ 

Here is Emrick's work.

$\begin{array}{rcl}
\cos^{-1}(\cos (x)) &= &\cos^{-1}( -0.13)\\\\
x &\approx &1.7\\\\
\swarrow&&\searrow\\\\
\begin{align}
&\blueE{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(x+360)}\\\\
x &\approx 1.7+  360n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -718.3\text{, }-358.3\text{,}1.7\ldots 
\end{align}&&
\begin{align}
&\purpleD{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(-x)}\\\\
&\purpleD{\text{and }\cos(x)=\cos(x+360)}\\\\
x &\approx -1.7 + 360 n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -361.7\text{, }-1.7\text{, }358.3\ldots 
\end{align}
\end{array}$

>The solutions to $x$ within the interval from $-360\degree$ to $0\degree$ are $-358.3\degree$ and $-1.7\degree$.

**What was Emrick's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Emrick needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse cosine function $\cos^{-1}$. He also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks him to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving cosine equations:

- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$
- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+360\degree)$

After Emrick uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, he should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $-360\degree$ to $0\degree$.

###Isolating $x$

$\cos^{-1}( -0.13)\approx 97.5\degree\approx 1.7\,\text{radians}$

Emrick isolated $x$ in radians instead of in degrees.

### Conclusion

Emrick evaluated $\cos^{-1}\left( -0.13\right)$ in radians instead of in degrees.

choices.content: Emrick evaluated $\cos^{-1}\left( -0.13\right)$ in radians instead of in degrees.
Emrick forgot to apply an identity which would reveal more solutions in the interval.
Emrick forgot to use larger $n$-values which would reveal another solution in the interval.
Emrick gave solutions that were outside of the interval.

assessment_items.question_content:
Caoimhe tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in radians to the nearest hundredth, in the interval from $\pi$ to $4\pi$, but she made a mistake.

$\sin(x)=0.71$ 

Here is Caoimhe's work.

$\begin{array}{rcl}
\sin^{-1}(\sin (x)) &= &\sin^{-1}( 0.71)\\\\
x &\approx &45.23\\\\
\swarrow&&\searrow\\\\
\begin{align}
&\blueE{\text{Using }\sin(x)=\sin(x+2\pi)}\\\\
x &\approx 45.23+  2\pi n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots 1.25\text{, }7.53\text{, }13.81\ldots 
\end{align}&&
\begin{align}
&\purpleD{\text{Using }\sin(x)=\sin(\pi-x)}\\\\
&\purpleD{\text{and }\sin(x)=\sin(x+2\pi)}\\\\
x &\approx -42.09 + 2\pi n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots 1.89\text{, }8.18\text{, }14.46\ldots 
\end{align}
\end{array}$

>The solutions to $x$ within the interval from $\pi$ to $4\pi$ are $7.53$ and $8.18$.

**What was Caoimhe's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Caoimhe needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse sine function $\sin^{-1}$. She also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks her to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving sine equations:

- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\pi-\theta)$
- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\theta+2\pi)$

After Caoimhe uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, she should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $\pi$ to $4\pi$.

###Isolating $x$

$\sin^{-1}( 0.71)\approx 45.23\degree\approx 0.79\,\text{radians}$

Caoimhe isolated $x$ in degrees instead of in radians.

### Conclusion

Caoimhe evaluated $\sin^{-1}\left( 0.71\right)$ in degrees instead of in radians.

choices.content: Caoimhe evaluated $\sin^{-1}\left( 0.71\right)$ in degrees instead of in radians.
Caoimhe forgot to apply an identity which would reveal another solution in the interval.
Caoimhe forgot to use negative $n$-values which would reveal another solution in the interval.
Caoimhe gave solutions that were outside of the interval.

assessment_items.question_content:
Andy tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in radians to the nearest hundredth, in the interval from $-2\pi$ to $3\pi$, but he made a mistake.

$\cos(x)=0.55$ 

Here is Andy's work.

$\begin{array}{rcl}
\cos^{-1}(\cos (x)) &= &\cos^{-1}( 0.55)\\\\
x &\approx &56.63\\\\
\swarrow&&\searrow\\\\
\begin{align}
&\blueE{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(x+2\pi)}\\\\
x &\approx 56.63+  2\pi n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -12.48\text{, }-6.20\text{, }0.08\text{, }6.37\text{, }12.65\ldots 
\end{align}&&
\begin{align}
&\purpleD{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(-x)}\\\\
&\purpleD{\text{and }\cos(x)=\cos(x+2\pi)}\\\\
x &\approx -56.63 + 2\pi n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -6.36\text{, }-0.08\text{, }6.20\text{, }12.49\ldots 
\end{align}
\end{array}$

>The solutions to $x$ within the interval from $-2\pi$ to $3\pi$ are $-6.2$, $-0.08$, $0.08$, $6.2$, and $6.37$.

**What was Andy's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Andy needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse cosine function $\cos^{-1}$. He also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks him to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving cosine equations:

- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$
- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+2\pi)$

After Andy uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, he should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $-2\pi$ to $3\pi$.

###Isolating $x$

$\cos^{-1}( 0.55)\approx 56.63\degree\approx 0.99\,\text{radians}$

Andy isolated $x$ in degrees instead of in radians.

### Conclusion

Andy evaluated $\cos^{-1}\left( 0.55\right)$ in degrees instead of in radians.

choices.content: Andy evaluated $\cos^{-1}\left( 0.55\right)$ in degrees instead of in radians.
Andy used an identity that only applies to the sine function, not to the cosine function.
Andy forgot to use larger $n$-values which would reveal another solution in the interval.
Andy gave solutions that were outside of the interval.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kelly tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in radians to the nearest hundredth, in the interval from $\pi$ to $4\pi$, but she made a mistake.

$\cos(x)=-0.35$

Here is Kelly's work.

$\begin{align}
\cos^{-1}(\cos(x))&=\cos^{-1}(-0.35)\\\\
x&=1.93\\\\
x&=1.93+2\pi n&n\text{ is any integer.}\\\\
x&\approx\ldots 1.93\text{, }8.21\text{, }14.50\ldots
\end{align}$

>Of the solutions, only $8.21$ is in the interval from $\pi$ to $4\pi$, so $x\approx 8.21$.

**What was Kelly's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Kelly needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse cosine function $\cos^{-1}$. She also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks her to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving cosine equations:

- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$
- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+2\pi)$

After Kelly uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, she should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $\pi$ to $4\pi$.

###Isolating $x$

$\cos^{-1}(-0.35) \approx1.93$

Kelly isolated $x$ in radians, which is correct.

###Applying identities

Kelly applied the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+2\pi)$, which revealed half of the possible solutions to the equation.

[[☃ image 1]]

However, Kelly forgot to apply the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$. So Kelly missed two more solutions within the interval.

### Conclusion

Kelly forgot to apply an identity which would reveal more solutions in the interval.

choices.content: Kelly evaluated $\cos^{-1}(-0.35)$ in degrees instead of in radians.
Kelly forgot to apply an identity which would reveal more solutions in the interval.
Kelly forgot to use negative $n$-values which would reveal more solutions in the interval.
Kelly gave solutions that were outside of the interval.

assessment_items.question_content:
Agustin tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in radians to the nearest hundredth, in the interval from $0$ to $3\pi$, but he made a mistake.

$\sin(x)=-0.81$

Here is Agustin's work.

$\begin{align}
\sin^{-1}(\sin(x))&=\sin^{-1}(-0.81)\\\\
x&=-0.94\\\\
x&=-0.94+2\pi n&n\text{ is any integer.}\\\\
x&\approx\ldots -0.94\text{, }5.34\text{, }11.62\ldots
\end{align}$

>Of the solutions, only $5.34$ is in the interval from $0$ to $3\pi$, so $x\approx 5.34$.

**What was Agustin's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Agustin needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse sine function $\sin^{-1}$. He also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks him to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving sine equations:

- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\pi-\theta)$
- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\theta+2\pi)$

After Agustin uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, he should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $0$ to $3\pi$.

###Isolating $x$

$\sin^{-1}(-0.81)\approx -0.94$

Agustin isolated $x$ in radians, which is correct.

###Applying identities

Agustin applied the identity $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\theta+2\pi)$, which revealed half of the possible solutions to the equation.

[[☃ image 1]]

However, Agustin forgot to apply the identity $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\pi-\theta)$. So Agustin missed one more solution within the interval.

### Conclusion

Agustin forgot to apply an identity which would reveal another solution in the interval.

choices.content: Agustin evaluated $\sin^{-1}(-0.81)$ in degrees instead of in radians.
Agustin forgot to apply an identity which would reveal another solution in the interval.
Agustin forgot to use negative $n$-values which would reveal more solutions in the interval.
Agustin gave solutions that were outside of the interval.

###Isolating $x$

$\cos^{-1}(0.74) \approx 42.3\degree$

Leila isolated $x$ in degrees, which is correct.

###Applying identities

Leila applied the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+360\degree)$, which revealed half of the possible solutions to the equation.

[[☃ image 1]]

However, Leila forgot to apply the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$. So Leila missed one more solution within the interval.

$\sin(x)=0.42$

Here is Duncan's work.

$\begin{align}
\sin^{-1}(\sin(x))&=\sin^{-1}(0.42)\\\\
x&=24.8\\\\
x&=24.8+360 n&n\text{ is any integer.}\\\\
x&\approx\ldots -335.2\text{, }24.8\text{, }384.8\text{, }744.8\ldots
\end{align}$

>The solutions to $x$ within the interval from $0\degree$ to $720\degree$ are $24.8\degree$ and $384.8\degree$.

**What was Duncan's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Duncan needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse sine function $\sin^{-1}$. He also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks him to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving sine equations:

- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(180\degree-\theta)$
- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\theta+360\degree)$

After Duncan uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, he should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $0\degree$ to $720\degree$.

###Isolating $x$

$\sin^{-1}(0.42)\approx 24.8\degree$

Duncan isolated $x$ in degrees, which is correct.

###Applying identities

Duncan applied the identity $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\theta+360\degree)$, which revealed half of the possible solutions to the equation.

However, Duncan forgot to apply the identity $\sin(\theta)=\sin(180\degree-\theta)$. So Duncan missed two more solutions within the interval.

Duncan forgot to apply an identity which would reveal more solutions in the interval.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lachlan tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in radians to the nearest hundredth, in the interval from $-\pi$ to $\pi$, but he made a mistake.

$\sin(x)=-0.25$ 

Here is Lachlan's work.

$\begin{array}{rcl}
\sin^{-1}(\sin (x)) &= &\sin^{-1}( -0.25 )\\\\
x &\approx &-0.25\\\\
\swarrow&&\searrow\\\\
\begin{align}
&\blueE{\text{Using }\sin(x)=\sin(x+2\pi)}\\\\
x &\approx -0.25 + 2\pi n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -6.53\text{, }-0.25\text{, }6.03\ldots 
\end{align}&&
\begin{align}
&\purpleD{\text{Using }\sin(x)=\sin(-x)}\\\\
&\purpleD{\text{and }\sin(x)=\sin(x+2\pi)}\\\\
x &\approx 0.25 + 2\pi n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -6.03\text{, }0.25\text{, }6.53\ldots 
\end{align}
\end{array}$

>The solutions to $x$ in the interval from $-\pi$ to $\pi$ are $x\approx -0.25$ and $x\approx 0.25$.

**What was Lachlan's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Lachlan needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse sine function $\sin^{-1}$. He also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks him to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving sine equations:

- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\pi-\theta)$
- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\theta+2\pi)$

After Lachlan uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, he should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $-\pi$ to $\pi$.

###Isolating $x$

$\sin^{-1}( -0.25 ) \approx -0.25$

Lachlan isolated $x$ in radians, which is correct.

###Applying identities

Lachlan applied the identity $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\theta+2\pi)$, which revealed half of the possible solutions to the equation.

[[☃ image 1]]

However, $\sin(\theta) \neq \sin(-\theta)$. Lachlan confused that with the cosine identity $\cos(\theta) = \cos(-\theta)$.

Instead, Lachlan should have applied the identity $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\pi-\theta)$.

### Conclusion

Lachlan used an identity that only applies to the cosine function, not to the sine function.

choices.content: Lachlan evaluated $\sin^{-1}\left( -0.25 \right)$ in degrees instead of in radians.
Lachlan used an identity that only applies to the cosine function, not to the sine function.
Lachlan forgot to use negative $n$-values which would reveal more solutions in the interval.
Lachlan gave a solution that was outside of the interval.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jamie tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in radians to the nearest hundredth, in the interval from $-\pi$ to $\pi$, but she made a mistake.

$\cos(x)=-0.08$ 

Here is Jamie's work.

$\begin{array}{rcl}
\cos^{-1}(\cos (x)) &= &\cos^{-1}( -0.08 )\\\\
x &\approx & 1.65\\\\
\swarrow&&\searrow\\\\
\begin{align}
&\blueE{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(x+2\pi)}\\\\
x &\approx 1.65+ 2\pi n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -4.63\text{, }1.65\text{, }7.93\ldots 
\end{align}&&
\begin{align}
&\purpleD{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(\pi-x)}\\\\
&\purpleD{\text{and }\cos(x)=\cos(x+2\pi)}\\\\
x &\approx 1.49 + 2\pi n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -4.79\text{, }1.49\text{, }7.77\ldots 
\end{align}
\end{array}$

>The solutions to $x$ in the interval from $-\pi$ to $\pi$ are $x\approx 1.49$ and $x\approx 1.65$.

**What was Jamie's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Jamie needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse cosine function $\cos^{-1}$. She also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks her to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving cosine equations:

- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$
- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+2\pi)$

After Jamie uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, she should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $-\pi$ to $\pi$.

###Isolating $x$

$\cos^{-1}( -0.08 ) \approx 1.65$

Jamie isolated $x$ in radians, which is correct.

###Applying identities

Jamie applied the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+2\pi)$, which revealed half of the possible solutions to the equation.

[[☃ image 1]]

However, $\cos(\theta) \neq \cos(\pi-\theta)$. Jamie confused that with the sine identity $\sin(\theta) = \sin(\pi-\theta)$.

Instead, Jamie should have applied the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$.

### Conclusion

Jamie used an identity that only applies to the sine function, not to the cosine function.

choices.content: Jamie evaluated $\cos^{-1}\left( -0.08 \right)$ in degrees instead of in radians.
Jamie used an identity that only applies to the sine function, not to the cosine function.
Jamie forgot to use negative $n$-values which would reveal more solutions in the interval.
Jamie gave solutions that were outside of the interval.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jonathan tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in degrees to the nearest tenth, in the interval from $0\degree$ to $360\degree$, but he made a mistake.

$\sin(x)=-0.66$ 

Here is Jonathan's work.

$\begin{array}{rcl}
\sin^{-1}(\sin (x)) &= &\sin^{-1}( -0.66 )\\\\
x &\approx &-41.3\\\\
\swarrow&&\searrow\\\\
\begin{align}
&\blueE{\text{Using }\sin(x)=\sin(x+360)}\\\\
x &\approx -41.3 + 360 n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -41.3\text{, }318.7\text{, }678.7\ldots 
\end{align}&&
\begin{align}
&\purpleD{\text{Using }\sin(x)=\sin(-x)}\\\\
&\purpleD{\text{and }\sin(x)=\sin(x+360)}\\\\
x &\approx 41.3 + 360 n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -318.7\text{, }41.3\text{, }401.3\ldots 
\end{align}
\end{array}$

>The solutions to $x$ in the interval from $0\degree$ to $360\degree$ are $x\approx 41.3$ and $x\approx 318.7$.

**What was Jonathan's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Jonathan needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse sine function $\sin^{-1}$. He also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks him to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving sine equations:

- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(180\degree-\theta)$
- $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\theta+360\degree)$

After Jonathan uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, he should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $0\degree$ to $360\degree$.

###Isolating $x$

$\sin^{-1}( -0.66 )\approx -41.3\degree$

Jonathan isolated $x$ in degrees, which is correct.

###Applying identities

Jonathan applied the identity $\sin(\theta)=\sin(\theta+360\degree)$, which revealed half of the possible solutions to the equation.

[[☃ image 1]]

However, $\sin(\theta) \neq \sin(-\theta)$. Jonathan confused that with the cosine identity $\cos(\theta) = \cos(-\theta)$.

Instead, Jonathan should have applied the identity $\sin(\theta)=\sin(180\degree-\theta)$.

### Conclusion

Jonathan used an identity that only applies to the cosine function, not to the sine function.

Jonathan used an identity that only applies to the cosine function, not to the sine function.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mckenna tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in degrees to the nearest tenth, in the interval from $-270\degree$ to $180\degree$, but she made a mistake.

$\cos(x)=0.56$ 

Here is Mckenna's work.

$\begin{array}{rcl}
\cos^{-1}(\cos (x)) &= &\cos^{-1}( 0.56)\\\\
x &\approx & 55.9\\\\
\swarrow&&\searrow\\\\
\begin{align}
&\blueE{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(x+360)}\\\\
x &\approx 55.9+ 360 n\\\\x &\approx \ldots -304.1\text{, }55.9\text{, }415.9\ldots 
\end{align}&&
\begin{align}
&\purpleD{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(180-x)}\\\\
&\purpleD{\text{and }\cos(x)=\cos(x+360)}\\\\
x &\approx 124.1 + 2\pi n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -595.9\text{, }-235.9\text{, }124.1\text{, }484.1\ldots 
\end{align}
\end{array}$

>The solutions to $x$ in the interval from $-270\degree$ to $180\degree$ are $-235.9\degree$, $55.9\degree$, and $124.1\degree$.

**What was Mckenna's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Mckenna needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse cosine function $\cos^{-1}$. She also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks her to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving cosine equations:

- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$
- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+360\degree)$

After Mckenna uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, she should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $-270\degree$ to $180\degree$.

###Isolating $x$

$\cos^{-1}( 0.56) \approx 55.9\degree$

Mckenna isolated $x$ in degrees, which is correct.

###Applying identities

Mckenna applied the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+360\degree)$, which revealed half of the possible solutions to the equation.

[[☃ image 1]]

However, $\cos(\theta) \neq \cos(180\degree-\theta)$. Mckenna confused that with the sine identity $\sin(\theta) = \sin(180\degree-\theta)$.

Instead, Mckenna should have applied the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$.

### Conclusion

Mckenna used an identity that only applies to the sine function, not to the cosine function.

choices.content: Mckenna evaluated $\cos^{-1}\left( 0.56\right)$ in radians instead of in degrees.
Mckenna used an identity that only applies to the sine function, not to the cosine function.
Mckenna forgot to use negative $n$-values which would reveal another solution in the interval.
Mckenna gave solutions that were outside of the interval.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kong tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in degrees to the nearest tenth, in the interval from $-360\degree$ to $90\degree$, but he made a mistake.

$\cos(x)=0.34$ 

Here is Kong's work.

$\begin{array}{rcl}
\cos^{-1}(\cos (x)) &= &\cos^{-1}( 0.34)\\\\
x &\approx &70.1\\\\
\swarrow&&\searrow\\\\
\begin{align}
&\blueE{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(x+360)}\\\\
x &\approx 70.1+ 360 n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots 70.1\text{, }430.1\ldots 
\end{align}&&
\begin{align}
&\purpleD{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(-x)}\\\\
&\purpleD{\text{and }\cos(x)=\cos(x+360)}\\\\
x &\approx -70.1 + 360 n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -70.1\text{, }289.9\ldots 
\end{align}
\end{array}$

>The solutions to $x$ within the interval from $-360\degree$ to $90\degree$ are $-70.1\degree$ and $70.1\degree$.

**What was Kong's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Kong needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse cosine function $\cos^{-1}$. He also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks him to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving cosine equations:

- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$
- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+360\degree)$

After Kong uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, he should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $-90\degree$ to $360\degree$.

###Isolating $x$

$\cos^{-1}( 0.34) \approx 70.1\degree$

Kong isolated $x$ in degrees, which is correct.

###Applying identities

Kong applied the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+360\degree)$, which revealed half of the possible solutions to the equation.

[[☃ image 1]]

Kong applied the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$, which revealed the other half of the solutions.

[[☃ image 2]]

### Listing potential solutions

Now Kong should adjust the value of $n$ to find all the values that fall within the desired interval. It is important to test higher and lower $n$-values to be sure that he has found all of the solutions.

Let us start with the first expression, $x=70.1+ 360 n$.

$n$ | $70.1+ 360 n$ | Within $[-360\degree, 90\degree]$?
:-: | :-: | :-:
$-2$ | $-649.9\degree$ | Too low
$-1$ | $-289.9\degree$ | **Yes**
$0$ | $70.1\degree$ | **Yes**
$1$ | $430.1\degree$ | Too high

Now the second expression, $x=-70.1+ 360 n$.

$n$ | $-70.1+ 360 n$ | Within $[-360\degree, 90\degree]$?
:-: | :-: | :-:
$-1$ | $-430.1\degree$ | Too low
$0$ | $-70.1\degree$ | **Yes**
$1$ | $289.9\degree$ | Too high

Kong left out the solution $x\approx -289.9\degree$ because he started the $n$-values too high.

### Conclusion

Kong forgot to use negative $n$-values which would reveal another solution in the interval.

choices.content: Kong evaluated $\cos^{-1}\left( 0.34\right)$ in radians instead of in degrees.
Kong used an identity that only applies to the sine function, not to the cosine function.
Kong forgot to use negative $n$-values which would reveal another solution in the interval.
Kong gave solutions that were outside of the interval.

Alba tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in degrees to the nearest tenth, in the interval from $0\degree$ to $630\degree$, but she made a mistake.

&\purpleD{\text{Using }\sin(x)=\sin(180-x)}\\\\
&\purpleD{\text{and }\sin(x)=\sin(x+360)}\\\\

x &\approx \ldots -111.6\text{, }248.4\ldots 
\end{align}

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]

Alba applied the identity $\sin(\theta)=\sin(180\degree-\theta)$, which revealed the other half of the solutions.

[[☃ image 2]]

### Listing potential solutions

Now Alba should adjust the value of $n$ to find all the values that fall within the desired interval. It is important to test higher and lower $n$-values to be sure that she has found all of the solutions.

Let us start with the first expression, $x=-68.4+ 360 n$.

$n$ | $-68.4+ 360 n$ | Within $[0\degree, 630\degree]$?

$-1$ | $-111.6$ | Too low
$0$ | $248.4$ | **Yes**
$1$ | $608.4$ | **Yes**
$2$ | $968.4$ | Too high

Alba left out the solution $x\approx 608.4\degree$ because she stopped the $n$-values too low.

### Conclusion

assessment_items.question_content:
Ryker tried to solve the following equation, where $x$ is in radians to the nearest hundredth, in the interval from $-4\pi$ to $0$, but he made a mistake.

$\cos(x)=0.8$ 

Here is Ryker's work.

$\begin{array}{rcl}
\cos^{-1}(\cos (x)) &= &\cos^{-1}( 0.8)\\\\
x &\approx &0.64\\\\
\swarrow&&\searrow\\\\
\begin{align}
&\blueE{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(x+2\pi)}\\\\
x &\approx 0.64+ 2\pi n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots 0.64\text{, }6.92\text{, }13.21\ldots 
\end{align}&&
\begin{align}
&\purpleD{\text{Using }\cos(x)=\cos(-x)}\\\\
&\purpleD{\text{and }\cos(x)=\cos(x+2\pi)}\\\\
x &\approx -0.64 + 2\pi n\\\\
x &\approx \ldots -0.64\text{, }5.64\text{, }11.93\ldots 
\end{align}
\end{array}$

>Of the solutions, only $-0.64$ is in the interval from $-4\pi$ to $0$, so $x\approx -0.64$.

**What was Ryker's mistake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Strategy

First, Ryker needs to isolate $x$ by using the inverse cosine function $\cos^{-1}$. He also needs to remember to match the function's units (radians or degrees) to what the equation asks him to find.

Then, there are two useful identities for solving cosine equations:

- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$
- $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+360\degree)$

After Ryker uses the identities to generalize to *all* solutions of the equation, he should decide which specific solutions are in the interval from $-4\pi$ to $0$.

###Isolating $x$

$\cos^{-1}( 0.8) \approx 0.64$

Ryker isolated $x$ in radians, which is correct.

###Applying identities

Ryker applied the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(\theta+2\pi)$, which revealed half of the possible solutions to the equation.

[[☃ image 1]]

Ryker applied the identity $\cos(\theta)=\cos(-\theta)$, which revealed the other half of the solutions.

[[☃ image 2]]

### Listing potential solutions

Now Ryker should adjust the value of $n$ to find all the values that fall within the desired interval. It is important to test higher and lower $n$-values to be sure that he has found all of the solutions.

Let us start with the first expression, $x=0.64+ 2\pi n$.

$n$ | $0.64+ 2\pi n$ | Within $[-4\pi, 0]$?
:-: | :-: | :-:
$-3$ | $-18.21\degree$ | Too low
$-2$ | $-11.92\degree$ | **Yes**
$-1$ | $-5.64\degree$ | **Yes**
$0$ | $0.64$ | Too high

Now the second expression, $x=-0.64+ 2\pi n$.

$n$ | $-0.64+ 2\pi n$ | Within $[-4\pi, 0]$?
:-: | :-: | :-:
$-2$ | $-13.21$ | Too low
$-1$ | $-6.92$ | **Yes**
$0$ | $-0.64$ | **Yes**
$1$ | $5.64$ | Too high

Ryker left out the solutions $x\approx -11.92$, $x\approx -6.92$, and $x\approx -5.64$ because he started the $n$-values too high.

### Conclusion

Ryker forgot to use negative $n$-values which would reveal more solutions in the interval.

choices.content: Ryker evaluated $\cos^{-1}\left( 0.8\right)$ in degrees instead of in radians.
Ryker used an identity that only applies to the sine function, not to the cosine function.
Ryker forgot to use negative $n$-values which would reveal more solutions in the interval.
Ryker gave solutions that were outside of the interval.

\end{align}
\end{array}$

### Listing potential solutions

Now Everleigh should adjust the value of $n$ to find all the values that fall within the desired interval. It is important to test higher and lower $n$-values to be sure that she has found all of the solutions.

Let us start with the first expression, $x=-0.28+ 2\pi$.

$n$ | $-0.28+ 2\pi$ | Within $[0, 2\pi]$?
:-: | :-: | :-:
$0$ | $-0.28$ | Too low
$1$ | $6.00$ | **Yes**
$2$ | $12.29$ | Too high

Now the second expression, $x=3.42+ 2\pi$.

$n$ | $3.42+ 2\pi$ | Within $[0, 2\pi]$?
:-: | :-: | :-:
$-1$ | $-2.86$ | Too low
$0$ | $3.42$ | **Yes**
$0$ | $9.70$ | Too high

Everleigh included the solution $x\approx -0.28$, even though it is too low for the interval.

### Conclusion

Everleigh gave a solution that was outside of the interval.

choices.content: Everleigh evaluated $\sin^{-1}\left( -0.28\right)$ in degrees instead of in radians.
Everleigh used an identity that only applies to the cosine function, not to the sine function.
Everleigh forgot to use negative $n$-values which would reveal another solution in the interval.
Everleigh gave a solution that was outside of the interval.
